schools.summary: The glorious figure: 'and I saw another mighty (strong) angel come down from heaven, clothed with a cloud: and a rainbow was upon his head, and his face was as it were the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire' — a majestic angel descending, wrapped in a cloud, the cloud, the rainbow, the sun-like face, and feet of fire marking his surpassing glory; the rainbow the emblem of God's covenant mercy, the sun-face dazzling brightness/glory (1:16), the fiery feet strength and judgment (1:15) — the angel uniting the tokens of divine majesty, covenant, and power; Barnes leans to a created angel of high rank, while many take the features as echoing the glorified Christ of ch. 1 (Barnes, Robertson).

Victorinus and Bede (with many) take the 'mighty angel' to represent Christ himself — the cloud, the rainbow of the covenant, the sun-face (1:16), and the fiery feet (1:15) echoing the vision of the glorified Lord; the angel is the Lord, or his special manifestation, descending in covenant mercy and judicial majesty to proclaim the consummation.

themes: another mighty angel come down from heaven, clothed with a cloud
a rainbow was upon his head, and his face was as it were the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire
the mighty angel clothed with a cloud and crowned with a rainbow
the tokens of covenant, glory, and judgment (read by many as Christ)

schools.summary: The open book and the posture: 'and he had in his hand a little book (biblaridion) open: and he set his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth' — the angel holding a little book now open (unlike the sealed scroll of ch. 5), the open book signifying a revelation now disclosed (its contents to be made known, the little book later given to John to eat, vv.9-10); and planting his feet on sea and land, bestriding both, claiming dominion or authority over the whole world (the posture signifying the universal scope of his message and the sovereignty of him whom he represents over all the earth) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: he had in his hand a little book open
he set his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth
the angel with the little open book, his feet on sea and land
the disclosed revelation and the universal claim

schools.summary: The mighty cry and the thunders: 'and cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth: and when he had cried, seven thunders uttered their voices' — the angel crying with a voice like a lion's roar (signifying power, majesty, and terror, cf. Amos 3:8; Hosea 11:10, the lion's roar the voice of authority and warning, the proclamation mighty and arresting); and in response seven thunders speaking ('seven' marking completeness, the thunders intelligible voices that John could understand and was about to write, v.4, their content however sealed); the thunders signifying weighty, complete divine utterances answering the angel's cry (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth
when he had cried, seven thunders uttered their voices
the angel's lion-like cry and the seven thunders
the mighty proclamation and the weighty divine utterances

schools.summary: The forbidden recording: 'and when the seven thunders had uttered their voices, I was about to write: and I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Seal up those things which the seven thunders uttered, and write them not' — John, having heard the thunders, about to write (understanding their words and preparing to record them, as commanded to write what he saw, 1:19, the thunders intelligible not mere noise); the heavenly voice forbidding him to record the thunders' words ('Seal up' — keep secret; 'write them not'), the words to be sealed, kept hidden, the only thing in the book deliberately concealed; the sealing marking that these particular revelations were not for disclosure (whether reserved for a later time, or kept secret to teach that not all of God's counsels are revealed, a limit to what John may communicate, some things God keeps hidden, Deuteronomy 29:29, the thunders' content remaining a mystery) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I was about to write: and I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Seal up those things which the seven thunders uttered
write them not
John forbidden to write the seven thunders' words
the deliberate concealment of a divine mystery

schools.summary: The gesture of an oath: 'and the angel which I saw stand upon the sea and upon the earth lifted up his hand to heaven' — the angel, bestriding sea and land (v.2), raising his hand toward heaven, the lifting of the hand the gesture of taking a solemn oath (cf. Daniel 12:7, the man clothed in linen lifting his hands to heaven and swearing; Genesis 14:22, Abraham); to 'lift up the hand to heaven' the universal posture of swearing (appealing to God in heaven as witness, the angel about to make a solemn affirmation under oath); the raised hand, joined to the oath that follows (v.6), giving the utmost solemnity to the angel's declaration about the consummation (what an angel affirms under oath, appealing to the eternal Creator, is sure and weighty) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: the angel which I saw stand upon the sea and upon the earth lifted up his hand to heaven
the angel lifting his hand to heaven to swear
the gesture of a solemn oath
the utmost solemnity of the coming declaration

schools.summary: The oath and its meaning: 'and sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth... and the sea... that there should be time no longer' — the angel swearing by the eternal, living God, Creator of all (the reference to God as everliving and Creator marking his sovereign power to determine the end and the certainty of what is sworn; Robertson notes the oath proves the angel is not Christ, who is sworn by, not swearing). On 'there should be time no longer' (hoti chronos ouketi estai), Barnes and Robertson (with Gill, Clarke, Stuart, Fuller) hold it does NOT mean the literal end of time/consummation (for the angel proceeds to speak of events still to come, v.7; ch. 11), but 'there shall be no more DELAY' — no further delay before the seventh trumpet sounds and the mystery of God is finished, the cry answering the saints' 'how long?' (6:10). Robertson: 'no more delay in the fulfillment of the seventh trumpet'; Clarke: 'no longer delay... no reference to the day of judgment.'

Vitringa (noted by Barnes) renders 'time shall be no more' (tempus non fore amplius) — but explains it not absolutely (as if at the seventh trumpet all things terminate) but restrictedly, that there would be no delay between the seventh trumpet's sounding and the fulfilment of the prophecies; the surface KJV reading ('time shall be no longer') taken by some as the literal end of time and the beginning of eternity, but the Reformed and critical consensus reads it of the end of delay, not the end of time itself.

debates.question: What does the angel's oath 'there should be time no longer' mean?

sides.position: 'No more delay' — no further delay before the seventh trumpet sounds and the mystery of God is finished (the dominant reading)
'Time shall be no more' — the literal end of time and the beginning of eternity (the surface KJV reading)
holders: some older interpreters (the absolute reading Vitringa and Barnes reject)

themes: sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven... and the earth... and the sea
that there should be time no longer
the angel's oath that there should be time no longer
no more delay before the consummation (vs the end of time)

schools.summary: The completion of the oath: 'but in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound, the mystery of God should be finished, as he hath declared to his servants the prophets' — the angel swearing that at the sounding of the seventh trumpet (now near, no more delay, v.6) the great consummation will come (the seventh trumpet marking the end); 'the mystery of God should be finished (etelesthe — completed, accomplished)' — the 'mystery of God' his hidden purpose, the whole plan of redemption and the establishment of his kingdom, long concealed, at the seventh trumpet fully accomplished (the secret counsel of God brought to completion); 'as he hath declared (euengelisen — announced as good news) to his servants the prophets' — God having foretold this consummation through the prophets, the completion of his mystery fulfilling all prophecy (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound, the mystery of God should be finished
as he hath declared to his servants the prophets
the mystery of God finished at the seventh trumpet
God's hidden purpose accomplished, fulfilling all prophecy

schools.summary: The new command: 'and the voice which I heard from heaven spake unto me again, and said, Go and take the little book which is open in the hand of the angel which standeth upon the sea and upon the earth' — the same heavenly voice (v.4) now bidding John go and take the open little book from the angel's hand (John called to receive the disclosed scroll); John to 'go and take' the book actively (the open scroll, whose contents are now to be made known, to be taken and eaten, vv.9-10, the prophet receiving the message before delivering it); the book taken from the world-bestriding angel (v.2, whose feet are on sea and land), the revelation coming through this majestic angel, John receiving it directly from the messenger of God's purpose (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Go and take the little book which is open in the hand of the angel which standeth upon the sea and upon the earth
John commanded to take the little open book from the angel
the prophet receiving the revelation before proclaiming it
the disclosed scroll from the world-bestriding angel

schools.summary: The command to eat the book (drawn from Ezekiel 2:8-3:3): 'and I went unto the angel, and said unto him, Give me the little book. And he said unto me, Take it, and eat it up; and it shall make thy belly bitter, but it shall be in thy mouth sweet as honey' — John obeying the heavenly command and approaching the angel for the scroll; to 'eat' the book being to receive its message inwardly, to assimilate the revelation, to make it part of oneself before proclaiming it (the prophet inwardly digesting God's word, as Ezekiel ate the scroll); the twofold effect being that the revelation is 'sweet as honey' in the mouth (the word of God precious, the privilege of prophecy delightful to receive) but bitter in the belly (the contents — judgments, woes, sufferings — bringing grief and pain when digested), the joy of receiving God's word joined to the sorrow of its solemn message (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: Take it, and eat it up; and it shall make thy belly bitter, but it shall be in thy mouth sweet as honey
John bidden to eat the book, sweet in the mouth but bitter in the belly
receiving and assimilating the revelation inwardly
the joy of God's word and the grief of its message of judgment

schools.summary: The fulfillment of the command: 'and I took the little book out of the angel's hand, and ate it up; and it was in my mouth sweet as honey: and as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter' — John taking and eating the book, experiencing exactly what the angel foretold (v.9): sweet in the mouth, bitter in the belly (the prophet inwardly receiving the revelation); 'sweet as honey' in the mouth the initial sweetness (the first reception of God's revelation delightful, to receive the word and be entrusted with prophecy and taste the truth being sweet, the joy of the prophetic gift); but 'my belly was bitter' once digested (the contents — the judgments, sufferings, and woes to be prophesied — bringing grief and pain, the sweetness of receiving giving way to the bitterness of the message, the prophet's joy in the word followed by sorrow over its solemn burden) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I took the little book out of the angel's hand, and ate it up; and it was in my mouth sweet as honey
as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter
John eating the book, finding it sweet then bitter
the joy of receiving God's word and the grief of its message

schools.summary: The renewed commission: 'and he said unto me, Thou must prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings' — having eaten the book, John recommissioned to prophesy further (the bitter-sweet revelation he has received he must now declare, the eating of the book equipping him for the prophesying that follows); 'prophesy again' meaning John must resume and continue his prophetic work (the little book's contents the substance of the further prophecies, chs. 11ff., the commission renewed after the interlude); 'before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings' marking the universal scope of the revelation (the prophecy concerning and addressing the whole world, the fourfold designation marking that the message is for all nations and rulers, not one people only) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Thou must prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings
John recommissioned to prophesy before many peoples and kings
the renewal and continuation of the prophetic work
the universal scope of the revelation
